did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses a customized deep learning framework called SEAA-UNet++ (an enhanced UNet++ architecture with Squeeze-and-Excitation blocks, Atrous Spatial Pyramid Pooling, and attention mechanisms) for melanoma segmentation in dermoscopic images. The model was trained and evaluated on the ISIC 2017 dataset, achieving an IoU of 74.56% and a Dice coefficient of 82.90%.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work proposing a customized deep learning framework (SEAA-UNet++) for melanoma segmentation. It details the methodology (including Squeeze-and-Excitation blocks, ASPP, and Test Time Augmentation), describes the training and evaluation on the ISIC 2017 dataset, and reports new quantitative results (IoU 74.56%, Dice 82.90%). Although published in a conference proceedings (IEEE STI 2025), it contains original research results rather than summarizing prior literature.

title: SEAA-UNet++: A Customized UNet++ Framework for Melanoma Segmentation in Dermoscopic Images with Test Time Augmentation
journal: 2025 IEEE 7th International Conference on Sustainable Technologies for Industry 5.0 (STI)
brief_summary: This study proposes SEAA-UNet++, a customized deep learning framework for melanoma segmentation in dermoscopic images, which enhances the UNet++ architecture with Squeeze-and-Excitation blocks, Atrous Spatial Pyramid Pooling, and attention mechanisms. The model was trained and evaluated on the ISIC 2017 dataset, utilizing preprocessing steps such as hair removal and Test Time Augmentation (TTA) to improve robustness and generalization. Experimental results demonstrated that the proposed method achieved an Intersection over Union (IoU) of 74.56%, a Dice coefficient of 82.90%, and a precision of 93.11%. Comparative analysis indicated that the model outperformed several state-of-the-art architectures, including UNet, UNet++, ATTU-Net, ResUNet++, and TC-Net, particularly in terms of precision and IoU. The findings suggest that integrating TTA with the enhanced architecture significantly improves segmentation performance and establishes a new standard for melanoma segmentation.

lesion_types_studied.details: The primary target for segmentation in the study. The paper focuses on segmenting melanoma lesions from dermoscopic images in the ISIC 2017 dataset. The segmentation masks define the lesion boundaries for melanoma.
Used as a general term for the objects being segmented. The study aims to segment skin lesions, specifically focusing on melanoma within the ISIC 2017 dataset context.

all_task_types.target: Melanoma lesion boundaries in dermoscopic images
all_task_types.details: The paper focuses on binary segmentation of melanoma lesions from dermoscopic images using the SEAA-UNet++ framework. The model predicts segmentation masks defining the lesion boundaries, evaluated on the ISIC 2017 dataset using metrics such as Intersection over Union (IoU), Dice Similarity Coefficient (DSC), Precision, and Recall. The task is explicitly described as 'precise dermoscopic skin lesion segmentation' and 'binary segmentation tasks'.

datasets_used.name: ISIC 2017
datasets_used.description: A dataset from the International Skin Imaging Collaboration initiative containing dermoscopic images of skin lesions with annotated binary masks defining lesion boundaries. The images were preprocessed (hair removal, resizing to 256x256) for use in the study.

use.training: Used for training the SEAA-UNet++ model (2000 images).
use.validation: Used for validation during training (150 images).
use.testing: Used for final evaluation of the model's performance (600 images).

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance comparisons were made using descriptive statistics (Dice Similarity Coefficient, Intersection over Union, Precision, and Recall) without statistical significance testing.

ISIC 2017.notes: The proposed SEAA-UNet++ with Test Time Augmentation (TTA) achieved the highest precision (93.11%) and IoU (74.56%) among the models evaluated in this study. It outperformed UNet, UNet++, ATTU-Net, ResUNet++, and TC-Net in precision and IoU, though it had slightly lower Dice scores than TC-Net, FAT-NET, and EIU-Net. The ablation study showed that integrating SE, ASPP, and Attention modules provided complementary advantages. Results were obtained over 50 epochs.

architecture_details.description: A customized UNet++ framework enhanced with SE, ASPP, and attention modules to improve feature representation at various scales. The architecture combines dense and stacked skip connections with a nested decoder design.

training_details.data_split: 90% training (2000 images), 10% validation (150 images) from ISIC 2017
preprocessing: Blackhat morphological treatment for hair removal
training_details.post_processing: Test Time Augmentation (TTA) applied during inference, involving flips, rotations, and zooms with averaging of predictions

classification_or_output_head.description: Decoder progressively restores spatial resolution of feature maps to create a final segmentation map for binary segmentation tasks.

preprocessing_techniques: Conversion of RGB images to grayscale
Blackhat morphological treatment for hair detection
Binary thresholding for artifact/hair mask separation

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Applied during inference on the test dataset to enhance model robustness and generalization. It involves generating diverse predictions by applying various transformations (flips, rotations, and zooms) to each test image and then averaging the individual segmentation results to produce a final, more reliable output. No additional training costs were incurred.

conclusions: The proposed SEAA-UNet++ framework, which integrates Squeeze-and-Excitation blocks, Atrous Spatial Pyramid Pooling, and attention mechanisms into the UNet++ architecture, effectively addresses challenges in melanoma segmentation such as varied lesion appearances, low contrast, and image aberrations like hair and shadows.

The integration of Test Time Augmentation (TTA) significantly improves the model's robustness, generalization, and segmentation accuracy by aggregating predictions from augmented test images without incurring additional training costs.

On the ISIC 2017 dataset, the SEAA-UNet++ model with TTA achieved an Intersection over Union (IoU) of 74.56%, a Dice coefficient of 82.90%, and a precision of 93.11%, demonstrating superior performance compared to the baseline UNet++ and several other state-of-the-art models including UNet, ATTU-Net, ResUNet++, and TC-Net.

Ablation studies confirmed that the combination of SE, ASPP, and Attention modules provides complementary advantages, with the full SEAA-UNet++ model outperforming variants with individual components.

While the proposed model achieved the highest precision and competitive IoU, it had slightly lower Dice scores compared to FAT-NET and EIU-Net, indicating that while it sets a new standard for precision and robustness, other architectures may achieve higher overall overlap metrics.
important_remarks: The study relies exclusively on the ISIC 2017 dataset for both training and evaluation, lacking external validation on independent datasets to assess generalizability.
No formal statistical hypothesis testing (e.g., p-values, confidence intervals) was performed; performance comparisons are based solely on descriptive statistics.

The proposed SEAA-UNet++ with TTA achieved a lower Dice Similarity Coefficient (82.90%) compared to several state-of-the-art models like EIU-Net (86.89%), FAT-NET (85.98%), and TC-Net (86.13%), indicating that while precision is high, overall overlap is not superior to all existing methods.

The model exhibits a trade-off between precision and recall; it achieves the highest precision (93.11%) but has a lower recall (80.67%) compared to models like ATTU-Net (88.66%), suggesting a tendency to minimize false positives at the cost of missing some lesion pixels.
The paper is a conference publication (IEEE STI 2025) with limited scope, potentially lacking the rigorous peer review and extensive ablation studies often found in top-tier journal articles.

Preprocessing steps, specifically hair removal via Blackhat morphological treatment and inpainting, were applied to training images but not explicitly detailed as being applied to test images in the same manner, raising potential concerns about data leakage or distribution shift if preprocessing parameters were derived from the test set.
The dataset split is fixed (90% training, 10% validation) without mention of cross-validation, which may lead to high variance in performance estimates depending on the specific split used.

The comparison with state-of-the-art models (e.g., EIU-Net, FAT-NET) uses reported metrics from other papers rather than re-running those models on the same split, which may introduce unfair comparisons due to differences in preprocessing, augmentation, or evaluation protocols.
